content.text: React's diffing algorithm determines the minimal set of DOM operations required to turn the previous render output into the new one. A naive comparison of two arbitrary trees is O(n³) — React reduces this to O(n) with two heuristics that hold for virtually all real UI structures: element type stability, and key-based identity for list items.

attrs.alt: React diffing two Fiber trees with key-based list reconciliation

content.text: The two heuristics work because UI components are structurally stable: a sidebar does not become a modal between renders, and list items carry stable business identifiers. React's algorithm is deliberately not general-purpose — it is tuned for the shape of real component trees.
Elements of different types produce completely different trees.
When the type of a node changes between renders, React unmounts the entire subtree rooted at that node and mounts a new one. It does not attempt to reuse DOM descendants.
This applies equally to host elements (DOM tags like
) and to component functions. If the

field of the virtual DOM node changes, the entire subtree is destroyed and rebuilt from scratch — including the state of all child components. This is true even if the two types produce visually identical output.

Defining components inside a render function creates a new function reference on every render. React treats it as a type change, unmounting and remounting on every parent re-render. Always define components at the module level.

When two consecutive renders produce nodes with the same type at the same tree position, React updates the existing DOM node rather than replacing it. For host elements it computes prop diffs; for components it re-renders them with the new props.
, etc.) React compares the old and new props objects and applies only the differences:
Updates the internal listener map; no DOM addEventListener/removeEventListener unless a new type
(not setAttribute) to avoid triggering DOM selection reset
prop React diffs the nested object, so
results in two DOM writes: set
. It does not serialize the entire style string.
Keys give the diffing algorithm a stable identity for each list item. React builds a map from key → Fiber for the current children, then walks the new children and looks up each key in that map:

The algorithm uses a single forward pass through the new children list. Items whose key exists in the current map are candidates for update (in-place if they land in the right position, moved otherwise). Items with new keys are insertions. Remaining entries in the current map are deletions. This keeps the algorithm linear — O(n) in the number of children.

Setting a different key on a component forces it to unmount and remount entirely — React treats a key change as a type change for identity purposes. This is occasionally useful as an intentional reset mechanism:
changes is simpler than writing a
that resets internal state. React throws away the old Fiber tree and builds a new one — state is fresh, all effects run again.
When React reconciles a set of children it walks through them in source order. A simplified view of the algorithm:

reconcileChildFibers(returnFiber, currentFirstChild, newChildren):

  1. Build a Map<key, Fiber> from current children
  2. For each new child (in order):
     a. If new child has a key:
        - Look it up in the map
        - If found: mark as UPDATE, remove from map
        - If not found: mark as INSERT
     b. If no key (positional):
        - Match by index against current sibling linked list
        - If same type: UPDATE
        - If different type or no current node: INSERT + DELETE current
  3. Any Fibers remaining in the map after step 2: DELETE

  Effects collected: Placement (INSERT), ChildDeletion (DELETE),
                     Update (same type, new props)

Using array index as key means every item has the same key as before at its position — React cannot distinguish a deletion from the front from a value change in place. For pure display lists that never reorder, indexes are safe. For any list where items can be added to the front, removed from the middle, or sorted, use stable IDs.
For component types, same type means React calls the component function (or
for classes) with the new props. React does not compare the component's output against the previous output first — it always calls the function.
Skip re-render if props are shallowly equal
Wraps function component; adds a bailout check before calling it
Class component method; checked before calling render()
Skips recomputation when deps are unchanged; does not skip the render
Prevents child bailouts from breaking when parent re-renders

React.memo only prevents re-rendering the memoised component. If it renders children, those children still re-render unless they are also memoised. Wrap the outermost component that receives stable props, not every leaf.
Under the hood the reconciler processes Fibers in a work loop. Each Fiber is processed in two passes:
top-down — compute the new virtual DOM output for this Fiber. Collect child Fibers to process next. Mark effects (Placement, Update, Deletion).
bottom-up — after all children are processed, build the real DOM instance (for host Fibers) or collect child effects into the parent.
During the render phase, as React processes each Fiber, it builds an

— a linked list of Fibers that need DOM work. During the commit phase it walks this list (which is a subset of the full tree) and applies the mutations in order: deletions first, then placements and updates.

Simplified commit order:

  Phase: commitBeforeMutationEffects
    → getSnapshotBeforeUpdate (class components)
    → useInsertionEffect cleanup (React 18)

  Phase: commitMutationEffects
    → process Deletion effects (remove DOM nodes, call cleanup effects)
    → process Placement effects (insert new DOM nodes)
    → process Update effects (apply prop diffs)
    → set / clear refs

  Phase: commitLayoutEffects
    → useLayoutEffect cleanup + setup
    → componentDidMount / componentDidUpdate

  (async, after browser paint)
  Phase: flushPassiveEffects
    → useEffect cleanup + setup
Position in the tree, not position in the source file, determines whether state is preserved. The following pattern is a common mistake:
The fix is to use a single component type with conditional props, or to use
to distinguish the two configurations explicitly — never to conditionally include entirely different component types at the same position.

React's diffing algorithm achieves O(n) complexity through two heuristics: element type identity (different type → full subtree replace) and key-based list reconciliation (stable keys → minimal insertions, updates, deletions). Understanding these rules explains why changing a component type resets all child state, why list keys are required for correctness, and how React avoids re-creating DOM nodes that are structurally unchanged.

How you structure conditional rendering significantly affects whether React considers nodes the same across renders. The position a node occupies in the returned JSX — not the condition itself — determines identity:
when the sibling following A should keep its position and state when A disappears.
Keys must be unique among siblings, but they do not need to be globally unique across the tree. Two different list components can each have a child with
without conflict. React scopes key lookup to the immediate parent.
Fragments can have keys, which is necessary when rendering a list where each item consists of multiple elements:
Nested lists each require their own key scope. A matrix (list of lists) needs keys at both the row level and the cell level:
shorthand fragments cannot have keys. Use
when the fragment needs an identity for reconciliation.
Certain patterns disable optimisations in the reconciler or cause unnecessary work:
New function type every render → full unmount on every parent re-render
React cannot know which props changed without comparing each one — may prevent memo bailout
Pass only the props the component actually uses
React cannot detect the change — no re-render triggered
New key every render → forced remount
Stale state on reordered items; incorrect DOM reuse

forces React to synchronously flush all pending state updates and render them before returning. It bypasses batching and causes a synchronous commit. Use it when a third-party library expects DOM updates to be immediate:

degrades performance by bypassing batching. Use it only when you need to read the DOM immediately after a state update — e.g. measuring layout after an append, or integrating with a third-party library that expects synchronous DOM updates.
) go through special reconciliation. React uses the DOM property

after each commit to detect user edits that React did not trigger. This allows the browser to handle IME composition (Japanese, Chinese, Korean input) correctly without React overwriting a partially-composed character.
When writing tests you sometimes need to verify that reconciliation behaved correctly — that a component instance was reused rather than remounted. React Testing Library's
function lets you re-render with different props into the same container:

) mark state updates as low priority. During a transition, React may render the new tree partially and then discard it if a higher-priority update arrives. This means a transition render may run multiple times before committing — another reason the render phase must be pure.
Because the transition render may be interrupted and restarted, do not put side-effects (
, analytics, subscriptions) directly in the render function — they would fire multiple times for what appears to be a single user action.
The practical rules that follow from the algorithm: never define components inside other component render functions, always use stable IDs as keys for dynamic lists, and use
only after profiling identifies a genuine bottleneck.
